categories.name: AI Framework Basics

Provide at least 3 examples for each core function.

categories.name: AI Attack Types
categories.intro: Adversarial machine learning (AML) is a subfield of cybersecurity focused on analyzing, anticipating, and preventing malicious attacks on AI systems. Discover the taxonomy of adversarial ML attacks and how NIST recommends defending against them.

questions.label: Provide me an overview of attacks grouped by predictive and generative AI.
questions.answer:
NIST AI 100-2e2023 provides a structured overview of adversarial machine learning attacks organized by AI system type and attacker objective.

PREDICTIVE AI — three objective dimensions:
- Availability: Model Poisoning [Model Control], Clean-Label Poisoning [Training Data Control + Label Limit], Data Poisoning [Training Data Control], Energy-Latency Attacks [Query Access]
- Integrity: Evasion [Test Data Control], Black-Box Evasion [Query Access], Clean-Label Backdoor [Training Data Control + Test Data Control + Label Limit], Clean-Label Poisoning [Training Data Control + Label Limit], Targeted Poisoning [Training Data Control], Backdoor Poisoning [Training Data Control + Test Data Control], Model Poisoning [Model Control]
- Privacy: Model Extraction [Query Access — Model], Data Reconstruction [Query Access — Data], Membership Inference [Query Access — Data], Property Inference [Query Access — Data]

GENERATIVE AI — four objective dimensions:
- Availability: Indirect Prompt Injection [Resource Control], Denial of Service [Resource Control], Prompt Injection [Query Access], Increased Computation [Query Access], Data Poisoning [Training Data Control]
- Integrity: Indirect Prompt Injection [Resource Control], Misaligned Inputs [Resource Control], Prompt Injection [Query Access], Targeted Poisoning [Training Data Control], Data Poisoning [Training Data Control], Backdoor Poisoning [Source Code Control]
- Abuse (GenAI-specific): Indirect Prompt Injection [Resource Control], Prompt Injection / Jailbreaking [Query Access] — techniques include prefix injection, role-play (DAN), special encoding, refusal suppression, phishing, masquerading, spreading malware, historical distortion
- Privacy — Model: Prompt Injection [Query Access], Prompt Extraction [Query Access]; Privacy — Data: Information Gathering [Query Access], Membership Inference [Query Access], Data Extraction [Query Access]; Privacy — Cross-Cutting: Backdoor Poisoning [Resource Control], Indirect Prompt Injection [Resource Control], Unauthorized Disclosure [Resource Control], Training Data Attacks [Resource Control]

categories.name: Secure AI Development Framework (SSDF)
categories.intro: Learn about how the SSDF Community Profile defines actions of secure AI model development for producers and enterprise buyers of AI models and systems. It applied therefore the standard software development life cycle (SDLC) categories—Prepare Organization (PO), Protect Software (PS), Produce Well-Secured Software (PW), and Respond to Vulnerabilities (RV).
questions: What is the scope of secure AI model development?
Summerize 10 measures of the SSDF Community Profile with their respective category IDs.

categories.name: Critical Infrastructure
categories.intro: Understand NIST's future trustworthiness requirements for AI in Critical Infrastructure (CI) across Information Technology (IT), Operational Technology (OT), and Industrial Control Systems (ICS)

questions.label: What are examples of AI systems that may be used in CI?
questions.answer:
Examples of AI systems that may be used in Critical Infrastructure, with features that can improve their trustworthiness, include but are not limited to:

- AI agents for autonomous cybersecurity incident response that include tested, evaluated, validated, and verified guardrails.
- AI-enabled facility and plant monitoring systems that are hardened against adversarial input and monitored for changes in the environment outside verified regions of validity.
- AI-enhanced deterministic diagnostic assistants that utilize AI bills of materials to provide traceable, auditable rationales for recommendations.
- Physics-informed neuro-symbolic AI systems for predicting and maintaining system stability that include verifiable performance guarantees.
- Autonomous robots and vehicles that leverage multimodal sensing, redundant safety systems, and deterministic fail-safe controllers.
- AI-powered digital twins for proactively managing distributed critical data centers to maintain operation during emergencies without overloading fragile utility infrastructure.
- AI optimization systems that degrade gracefully and transparently in response to adverse conditions while alerting human supervisors to take additional measures.
- AI-enabled, transparent, and explainable compliance and risk monitoring systems to improve governance responsiveness while maintaining human-in-the-loop oversight.

questions: Which stakeholders are invited to contribute to the current development of the NIST AI RMF Trustworthy AI in CI?
